summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

A data set with no outliers has a range of 15. A value of 200 is added. What is the most likely effect?

Adding 200 (likely much higher than the current max) makes it the new max, dramatically increasing the range.

What is an 'outlier'?

An outlier is a data value that lies far away from the rest of the data, either much higher or much lower than the majority of values.

A teacher recorded test scores for 10 students: 78, 82, 80, 85, 79, 83, 81, 84, 80, 35. The mean without the score of 35 is 81.3. What is the mean with 35 included, and how much did the outlier change it?

Sum with 35: 78+82+80+85+79+83+81+84+80+35 = 767. Mean = 767/10 = 76.7. The outlier decreased the mean from 81.3 to 76.7, a drop of 4.6 points.

A store tracked daily customers: 85, 90, 88, 92, 87, 91, 250 (holiday sale). The manager wants to predict normal daily traffic. What is the best approach?

The holiday sale created an outlier (250). For normal daily traffic, the median (90) or the mean excluding the outlier (88.8) gives a better prediction.

summary_sections.title: Calculation Methods
summary_sections.content_html:
How to Calculate

Example: The data set {20, 22, 24, 25, 23} has a mean of 22.8. If 100 is added, the new mean becomes approximately:

Solution: New sum = 20 + 22 + 24 + 25 + 23 + 100 = 214. New mean = 214 / 6 ≈ 35.7. The outlier raised the mean from 22.8 to about 35.7.

Example: Data set: {5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12}. The mean is about 8.86. If the value 5 is removed, what happens to the mean?

Solution: Removing the lowest value (5) increases the mean. New sum = 57, new count = 6, new mean = 57/6 = 9.5, up from 8.86.

Example: A student scored 95, 88, 92, 90, 91 on five tests. She accidentally entered a 9 instead of 90 for one test in her spreadsheet, making the data {95, 88, 92, 9, 91}. How does this error affect the mean?

Solution: Correct mean: (95+88+92+90+91)/5 = 456/5 = 91.2. Error mean: (95+88+92+9+91)/5 = 375/5 = 75. The data entry error (creating an outlier of 9) dropped the mean by 16.2 points.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

Most values cluster between 12 and 16, but 50 is far from the rest, making it an outlier.

The mean uses every value in its calculation, so an extreme outlier will pull the mean significantly toward it.

IQR = 20 - 10 = 10. Upper fence = 20 + 1.5(10) = 35. Lower fence = 10 - 1.5(10) = -5. Since 36 > 35, it is an outlier.

The median is resistant to outliers because it depends only on the middle value(s), not on the magnitude of extreme values.

A high outlier becomes the new maximum, increasing the difference between the max and min, which increases the range.

The range is calculated using the max and min, so a single outlier directly changes the range. The IQR is resistant because it only uses Q1 and Q3.

Adding 200 (likely much higher than the current max) makes it the new max, dramatically increasing the range.

An outlier is a data value that lies far away from the rest of the data, either much higher or much lower than the majority of values.

A resistant statistic is one that is not significantly affected by extreme values or outliers. The median and IQR are resistant; the mean and range are not.

The upper fence is calculated as Q3 + 1.5 x IQR. Any data value above this fence is classified as a potential upper outlier.

The lower fence is Q1 - 1.5 x IQR. Any data value below this fence is classified as a potential lower outlier.

When an outlier is present, it 'pulls' the mean toward its value—a high outlier increases the mean and a low outlier decreases it.

Outliers can create or increase skewness in a distribution by stretching one tail, making it longer than the other.

A non-resistant measure is significantly affected by outliers or extreme values. The mean and range are non-resistant because they use every value.

Identifying outliers means using a systematic method (often the 1.5 x IQR rule) to determine which values are far enough from the bulk of the data to be considered outliers.

Sum with 35: 78+82+80+85+79+83+81+84+80+35 = 767. Mean = 767/10 = 76.7. The outlier decreased the mean from 81.3 to 76.7, a drop of 4.6 points.

Ordered: {49.8, 49.9, 50.0, 50.1, 50.1, 50.2, 50.3, 55.0}. Q1 = 49.95, Q3 = 50.25. IQR = 0.30. Upper fence = 50.25 + 1.5(0.30) = 50.70. Since 55.0 > 50.70, it is an outlier.

Ordered: {45, 71, 72, 73, 74, 75, 76}. Median = 73 (barely changed from what it would be without 45). Mean = 486/7 ≈ 69.4 (dropped significantly). The mean is much more affected.

Since 120 bpm was due to exercise (not a true resting rate), the doctor should either use the median (which is resistant) or exclude the outlier with an explanation.

Lower fence = Q1 - 1.5(IQR) = 65 - 1.5(20) = 65 - 30 = 35. Since 32 < 35, the score of 32 is below the lower fence and is an outlier.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A teacher recorded test scores for 10 students: 78, 82, 80, 85, 79, 83, 81, 84, 80, 35. The mean without the score of 35 is 81.3. What is the mean with 35 included, and how much did the outlier change it?

Sum with 35: 78+82+80+85+79+83+81+84+80+35 = 767. Mean = 767/10 = 76.7. The outlier decreased the mean from 81.3 to 76.7, a drop of 4.6 points.

Example: A real estate agent listed 8 home prices in a neighborhood (in thousands): $180, $195, $200, $210, $205, $190, $185, $750. A buyer asks for the 'average' home price. Should the agent use the mean or median, and why?

The $750K home is a clear outlier. The median ($197.50) better represents a typical home price than the mean ($264.375K), which is inflated by the outlier.

Example: A student scored 95, 88, 92, 90, 91 on five tests. She accidentally entered a 9 instead of 90 for one test in her spreadsheet, making the data {95, 88, 92, 9, 91}. How does this error affect the mean?

Correct mean: (95+88+92+90+91)/5 = 456/5 = 91.2. Error mean: (95+88+92+9+91)/5 = 375/5 = 75. The data entry error (creating an outlier of 9) dropped the mean by 16.2 points.

Example: A store tracked daily customers: 85, 90, 88, 92, 87, 91, 250 (holiday sale). The manager wants to predict normal daily traffic. What is the best approach?

The holiday sale created an outlier (250). For normal daily traffic, the median (90) or the mean excluding the outlier (88.8) gives a better prediction.

Example: A data set of exam scores has Q1 = 65, Q3 = 85, and IQR = 20. A student scored 32. Using the 1.5 x IQR rule, is 32 an outlier?

Lower fence = Q1 - 1.5(IQR) = 65 - 1.5(20) = 65 - 30 = 35. Since 32 < 35, the score of 32 is below the lower fence and is an outlier.
